## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

  ## Character
  Iterate-until-it-works — unknown-discovery zone, bounded. If cycle count
  exceeds ~3-4 without progress, wrap with findings and chivvy a fresh design
  pace rather than accreting.
  
  ## Docket
  Make garrison-w complete step 6 round-trip end-to-end against the
  committed code from the redesign+implement pace.
  
  Each cycle = mechanism + one targeted repair + run + diagnose. Not
  speculative cascading fixes.
  
  Done when:
  - tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh bujn-winpc returns exit 0 with workload
    identity bujuw_user.
  - tt/buw-jwc and tt/buw-jws end-to-end operations succeed against the
    same node.
  - BUSJGW still coherent against implemented behavior.
  - Diag instrumentation decision recorded (kept-with-note vs pruned).
  
  Out of scope: c-letter (Cygwin) inherited defect — separate pace
  post-w-confirmation.

content.content:
1	## Goal
2	
3	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
4	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
5	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
6	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
7	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
8	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
9	
10	## Current Concept
11	
12	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
13	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
14	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing all three verbs).
15	Neither the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing
16	in BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
17	
18	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
19	
20	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
21	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
22	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
23	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
24	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
25	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
26	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
27	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
28	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
29	
30	**BUJB constant:**
31	
32	- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
33	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
34	  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.
35	
36	**Three verbs (Caparison, Invigilate, Garrison):**
37	
38	- **Caparison** — admin host posture establishment, per platform
39	  (Windows/macOS/Linux). Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes
40	  the caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
41	  Operator handbook owns security-sensitive edits (UAC-required registry
42	  keys, Tailscale interactive auth, etc.); caparison owns idempotent
43	  admin-shell operations.
44	- **Invigilate** — read-only host posture verification, per platform. Pure
45	  precondition assertions; no mutations. Each posture fact has a read
46	  command, expected value, and failure pointer to either the operator
47	  handbook (for operator-managed posture) or caparison (for
48	  caparison-managed posture). Single source of truth for "correctly-configured"
49	  per platform. Used twice: caparison's post-completion check, garrison's
50	  precondition check.
51	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per shell-letter
52	  (b/c/w). First step is invigilate-{platform}; failure surfaces immediately.
53	  Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
54	  authorized_keys.
55	
56	**Garrison-destructive model:**
57	
58	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
59	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
60	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
61	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
62	- Admin-side host posture is caparison's scope; the security-sensitive
63	  remainder is the per-platform handbook's scope.
64	
65	**Cross-cutting premises:**
66	
67	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
68	  it by reference, never duplicates.
69	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
70	  material; operator owns all key administration.
71	
72	**Tabtarget colophons (23 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
73	
74	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
75	- `buw-jpC[WML]` — Caparison per platform (W=Windows; M=macOS; L=Linux) — 3
76	- `buw-jpI[WML]` — Invigilate per platform — 3
77	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
78	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
79	- `buw-jpS` — Privileged SSH (admin pass-through, all platforms) — 1
80	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
81	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
82	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
83	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top (dispatches to per-platform) — 1
84	- `buw-hj[WML]` — Handbook per platform — 3
85	
86	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
87	
88	BCG-compliant module housing caparison, invigilate, and garrison
89	implementations, hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
90	mappings (b/c/w) used by garrison on the workload account, the workload
91	privkey destination path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL
92	distribution name (`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config`
93	hardening directive set used by caparison-windows. SSH transport mechanics
94	live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.
95	
96	**Caparison-Windows ceremony (admin posture; Windows OpenSSH host):**
97	
98	Three-phase: SSH-trust hardening (former fenestrate scope), then post-trust
99	admin posture, then post-completion verification.
100	
101	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
102	
103	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
104	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
105	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
106	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Caparison
107	   process never sees the password.
108	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
109	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
110	   (no `command=`; uniform across shell-letters). Performed before any
111	   sshd_config change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
112	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
113	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
114	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
115	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
116	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
117	   values.
118	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
119	   malformed config aborts caparison before it could brick running sshd).
120	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
121	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
122	   expected phase boundary).
123	
124	Phase 2 (key auth only):
125	
126	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
127	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
128	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
129	   a session.
130	9. Verify post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.
131	
132	Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):
133	
134	10. Stage `rbtww-main` WSL distribution under admin user via `wsl --import`
135	    from Ubuntu-24.04 seed. Always purge-and-reimport; no skip-if-present.
136	11. Apply powercfg sleep/hibernate disable (idempotent).
137	12. Set Tailscale service StartupType Automatic; start service
138	    (idempotent).
139	13. Run invigilate-windows post-completion check; non-zero indicates
140	    caparison's operations did not take or operator-managed posture is
141	    wrong.
142	
143	**Caparison-macOS ceremony (admin posture; macOS host):**
144	
145	Compact: enable Remote Login (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), orchestrate
146	ssh-copy-id, apply `pmset` power config, ensure Tailscale service
147	auto-start. Closes with invigilate-macos post-check.
148	
149	**Caparison-Linux ceremony (admin posture; Linux host):**
150	
151	Compact: orchestrate ssh-copy-id, mask sleep/suspend/hibernate targets via
152	systemctl (laptop/desktop hosts only), enable tailscaled. Closes with
153	invigilate-linux post-check.
154	
155	**Invigilate ceremony (per platform; read-only):**
156	
157	Each invigilate-{platform} reads its posture fact list and asserts each
158	fact matches expected. Failure surfaces a diagnostic naming the wrong fact
159	and pointing to either the operator handbook (for operator-managed
160	posture) or caparison (for caparison-managed posture). No mutations, no
161	remediation. Invocation contexts: caparison's post-completion check,
162	garrison's precondition check.
163	
164	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
165	
166	1. Invigilate-{platform} precondition check; failure surfaces a missing or
167	   stale admin posture before any destructive workload work.
168	2. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
169	   only (no password fallback).
170	3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
171	   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
172	   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
173	4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
174	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
175	5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
176	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
177	6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
178	   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can
179	   authenticate outbound to GitHub).
180	7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
181	
182	## Heat Sequence
183	
184	**Spec foundation:**
185	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
186	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
187	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (since broadened to
188	  caparison)
189	
190	**Implementation:**
191	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
192	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, fenestrate + garrison ceremonies, cleanup
193	  superseded colophons + handbook entries
194	
195	**Caparison/Invigilate restructuring:**
196	- BUS0 vocabulary + skeleton stubs (BUSJPF→BUSJCW; new BUSJC[ML],
197	  BUSJI[WML], BUSJH[WML])
198	- Per-platform caparison and invigilate spec content (Windows depth first,
199	  then macOS/Linux)
200	- Bash alignment via comment annotations (no symbol renames; preserves
201	  grep-archaeology for the upcoming spec/implementation review cycles)
202	
203	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
204	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
205	
206	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
207	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
208	- Garrison-WSL exercise
209	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
210	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
211	
212	## Deferred (named for future return)
213	
214	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
215	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
216	  only.
217	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
218	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
219	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
220	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
221	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
222	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
223	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
224	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
225	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
226	- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
227	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
228	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
229	  caparison phase or sibling verb.
230	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
231	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
232	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
233	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
234	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
235	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
236	  a time, never both running concurrently).
237	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
238	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
239	
240	## Standing Notes
241	
242	- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
243	  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
244	  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
245	  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
246	  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
247	  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
248	  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
249	  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
250	  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
251	  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
252	  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
253	- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
254	  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
255	  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
256	  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
257	  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
258	- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
259	  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
260	  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
261	  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
262	  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
263	  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
264	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
265	  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
266	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
267	  privkey-at-rest protection.
268	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
269	  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
270	  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
271	  precondition.
272	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
273	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
274	  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
275	  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
276	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
277	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
278	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
279	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
280	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
281	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
282	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
283	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
284	  and path construction.
285	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
286	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
287	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
288	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
289	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
290	  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
291	- **Mac Remote Login enabled is an operator prerequisite for any Mac BURN
292	  target** (including the operator's own machine). The first admin SSH
293	  must reach the host before caparison can do anything; only the operator
294	  can flip Remote Login on a fresh machine.
295	- **Linux nodes require `openssh-server` installed and `sshd` enabled** —
296	  operator-managed, not in caparison's scope.
297	- **Sudo NOPASSWD for `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` is an operator-managed
298	  precondition** wherever garrison needs sudo elevation. On macOS,
299	  admin-group membership for `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` is also operator-
300	  managed (equivalent in posture to the ssh-copy-id prerequisite).
301	  Blanket `NOPASSWD: ALL` acceptable on personal workstations; scoped
302	  is preferred for shared hosts.
303	- **Workload shell is `/bin/bash` on every platform** regardless of
304	  platform default. macOS ships /bin/bash 3.2 at the canonical path —
305	  bash is the BUK execution substrate everywhere.

file.content:
## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing all three verbs).
Neither the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing
in BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BUJB constant:**

- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.

**Three verbs (Caparison, Invigilate, Garrison):**

- **Caparison** — admin host posture establishment, per platform
  (Windows/macOS/Linux). Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes
  the caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
  Operator handbook owns security-sensitive edits (UAC-required registry
  keys, Tailscale interactive auth, etc.); caparison owns idempotent
  admin-shell operations.
- **Invigilate** — read-only host posture verification, per platform. Pure
  precondition assertions; no mutations. Each posture fact has a read
  command, expected value, and failure pointer to either the operator
  handbook (for operator-managed posture) or caparison (for
  caparison-managed posture). Single source of truth for "correctly-configured"
  per platform. Used twice: caparison's post-completion check, garrison's
  precondition check.
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per shell-letter
  (b/c/w). First step is invigilate-{platform}; failure surfaces immediately.
  Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side host posture is caparison's scope; the security-sensitive
  remainder is the per-platform handbook's scope.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (23 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpC[WML]` — Caparison per platform (W=Windows; M=macOS; L=Linux) — 3
- `buw-jpI[WML]` — Invigilate per platform — 3
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jpS` — Privileged SSH (admin pass-through, all platforms) — 1
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top (dispatches to per-platform) — 1
- `buw-hj[WML]` — Handbook per platform — 3

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing caparison, invigilate, and garrison
implementations, hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
mappings (b/c/w) used by garrison on the workload account, the workload
privkey destination path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL
distribution name (`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config`
hardening directive set used by caparison-windows. SSH transport mechanics
live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.

**Caparison-Windows ceremony (admin posture; Windows OpenSSH host):**

Three-phase: SSH-trust hardening (former fenestrate scope), then post-trust
admin posture, then post-completion verification.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Caparison
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; uniform across shell-letters). Performed before any
   sshd_config change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values.
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts caparison before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.

Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):

10. Stage `rbtww-main` WSL distribution under admin user via `wsl --import`
    from Ubuntu-24.04 seed. Always purge-and-reimport; no skip-if-present.
11. Apply powercfg sleep/hibernate disable (idempotent).
12. Set Tailscale service StartupType Automatic; start service
    (idempotent).
13. Run invigilate-windows post-completion check; non-zero indicates
    caparison's operations did not take or operator-managed posture is
    wrong.

**Caparison-macOS ceremony (admin posture; macOS host):**

Compact: enable Remote Login (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), orchestrate
ssh-copy-id, apply `pmset` power config, ensure Tailscale service
auto-start. Closes with invigilate-macos post-check.

**Caparison-Linux ceremony (admin posture; Linux host):**

Compact: orchestrate ssh-copy-id, mask sleep/suspend/hibernate targets via
systemctl (laptop/desktop hosts only), enable tailscaled. Closes with
invigilate-linux post-check.

**Invigilate ceremony (per platform; read-only):**

Each invigilate-{platform} reads its posture fact list and asserts each
fact matches expected. Failure surfaces a diagnostic naming the wrong fact
and pointing to either the operator handbook (for operator-managed
posture) or caparison (for caparison-managed posture). No mutations, no
remediation. Invocation contexts: caparison's post-completion check,
garrison's precondition check.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. Invigilate-{platform} precondition check; failure surfaces a missing or
   stale admin posture before any destructive workload work.
2. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback).
3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can
   authenticate outbound to GitHub).
7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (since broadened to
  caparison)

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, fenestrate + garrison ceremonies, cleanup
  superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Caparison/Invigilate restructuring:**
- BUS0 vocabulary + skeleton stubs (BUSJPF→BUSJCW; new BUSJC[ML],
  BUSJI[WML], BUSJH[WML])
- Per-platform caparison and invigilate spec content (Windows depth first,
  then macOS/Linux)
- Bash alignment via comment annotations (no symbol renames; preserves
  grep-archaeology for the upcoming spec/implementation review cycles)

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  caparison phase or sibling verb.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
  precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
  and path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
- **Mac Remote Login enabled is an operator prerequisite for any Mac BURN
  target** (including the operator's own machine). The first admin SSH
  must reach the host before caparison can do anything; only the operator
  can flip Remote Login on a fresh machine.
- **Linux nodes require `openssh-server` installed and `sshd` enabled** —
  operator-managed, not in caparison's scope.
- **Sudo NOPASSWD for `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` is an operator-managed
  precondition** wherever garrison needs sudo elevation. On macOS,
  admin-group membership for `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` is also operator-
  managed (equivalent in posture to the ssh-copy-id prerequisite).
  Blanket `NOPASSWD: ALL` acceptable on personal workstations; scoped
  is preferred for shared hosts.
- **Workload shell is `/bin/bash` on every platform** regardless of
  platform default. macOS ships /bin/bash 3.2 at the canonical path —
  bash is the BUK execution substrate everywhere.

lastPrompt: mount A-AA5

content.text:
## ₢A-AA5 (bash-spec-link-comments)

**Goal**: Add one-line comment headers to public bash functions pointing at their new spec section homes — no renames, just grep-able spec→code links to support upcoming review cycles.

**Heat context** (`₣A-` rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures): The heat sequence has spec restructuring largely complete (BUSJ family: caparison/invigilate/handbook/garrison × W/M/L + fenestrate/privileged-ssh). This pace is the bash-side alignment — comment annotations bind existing symbols to new spec sections without renaming, preserving grep archaeology for the review cycles ahead. Recent work: BUS0 trim (₢A-AAy), BUJP preflight mint (₢A-AAy).

**Docket**:

```
## Character
Light comment-only bash touch — preserves chain of custody for review cycles.

## Goal
Bind existing bash function symbols to new spec sections via comment headers
without renaming any symbol. Greppability holds still through subsequent
review cycles.

## Boundary
- One-line comment near each affected function naming its new spec section
  (e.g., `# Implements caparison-windows §SSH-trust phases; see BUSJPCw.`).
- Comment annotations on zipper enrollment lines if they help spec→code lookup.
- No symbol renames: functions, constants, env vars, file names all stay.
- `bujb_fenestrate` stays `bujb_fenestrate`. `bujb_garrison_wsl` stays.
  `BUJB_command_b/c/w` constants stay named as-is.

## Done when
- Every public bash function whose spec home moved or was newly created has
  a comment header pointing at the new spec.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.
```

**Name assessment**: `bash-spec-link-comments` fits — describes the operation (comment-only annotation) and the direction (bash → spec link).

**Note on order**: The recommended next was ₢A-AA1 (empirical-garrison-w-workload-wsl, marked rough). You're picking A-AA5 ahead of it — fine, just flagging in case it was a typo.

**Proposed approach**:
- Inventory affected modules: `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` (caparison/invigilate/garrison verbs), `bujp_preflight.sh` (just minted), `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` + `buhw_windows.sh` (handbook), and the `buw-jp*` / `buw-hj*` tabtarget colophons via zipper enrollments.
- For each public function, identify which BUS0 sub-spec it implements (BUSJC{W,M,L} / BUSJI{W,M,L} / BUSJH{W,M,L} / BUSJG{B,C,W} / BUSJPF / BUSJPS / etc.) and add a single `# Implements <verb-platform>; see <ACRONYM>.` line above the function.
- Annotate zipper enrollment rows where the spec target is non-obvious.
- Gate: `bash -n` on touched files, `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh`, `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`.

**Execution strategy**: Sequential sonnet — comment-only edits are mechanical, but each function's spec target requires a small lookup (grep the BUSJ* docs to confirm which sub-spec hosts that verb-platform). One coherent pass with one operator beats parallelizing micro-edits.

Ready to proceed?

content: Mounting pace ₢A-AA5 (bash-spec-link-comments) in heat ₣A- — adding one-line comment headers to public bash functions pointing at their new BUSJ* spec sections, no renames. Next: inventory affected modules and confirm you want to proceed.

a37d1d65 jjb:1011-311da14a:₢A-AAy:W: Minted BUJP preflight module (Tools/buk/bujp_preflight.sh) as garrison's precondition layer; bujb_garrison dispatcher now calls bujp_preflight at step 1 in place of the implicit SSH-connect probe (zbujb_garrison_step1_admin_open removed; zbujb_garrison_w_preflight migrated as zbujp_probe_wsl_distribution). BUJP dispatches per (shell-letter, BURN_PLATFORM): universal SSH-connectivity via zbujb_admin_exec_*; b-linux adds sudo NOPASSWD probe (sudo -n true baseline + sudo -ln userdel scope); b-mac adds dseditgroup admin-group probe then sudo NOPASSWD probe (sysadminctl scope); c-windows SSH-only (fenestrate covers Windows-admin elevation); w-windows adds WSL distribution presence probe. Sudo-probe failure routes to a failure-recovery flow: render a scoped /etc/sudoers.d/bujb-garrison snippet locally, scp to ~/.cache/bujb-garrison/ on remote, validate over SSH via visudo -cf, then buc_die with a copy-paste-safe 'sudo install -m 0440' line referencing the staged file (plus a blanket-NOPASSWD alternative for personal workstations). BUS0 reassigns sub-letter `p` from the withdrawn PowerShell-sibling reservation to preflight (verified clean: no active bujp_*/BUJP_* symbols in code, no bujp-* directories in .buk/, gallops `bujp-` mention was retrospective only — only `bujn-` materialized); adds the bujp_preflight quoin (mapping + anchor + definition under §Remote Node Access); updates busn_garrison body to name b-letter flavor branching as load-bearing + the b/c/w elevation matrix (b: sudo -n on linux+mac; c: rides on caparison's Windows-admin key trust; w: wsl.exe --user root, no sudo) + uniform step-1 preflight gate via bujp_preflight. CLAUDE.md gains a BUJP row. Paddock landed four new standing notes — Mac Remote Login operator-prerequisite, Linux openssh-server operator-managed, sudo NOPASSWD + macOS admin-group membership operator-managed (analogous to ssh-copy-id), workload shell /bin/bash forced on every platform — via two jjx_paddock commits (0aa52995 initial, 1e3e1c10 trim). Mid-pace book-report critique surfaced extensive DRY/word-cancer violations across file headers, function comments, BUS0 definition body, paddock note tails, and CLAUDE.md entry; trimmed in two passes: (1) collapsed bujp_preflight.sh file header from ~30 lines to one, reduced function comments to terse contracts (dropped probe-before-stage rationale, pace-history references, probe-matrix duplications), stripped BUS0 command-list enumeration (`userdel -r` vs `sysadminctl -deleteUser` etc.) keeping only the load-bearing claim that step bodies branch on BURN_PLATFORM, collapsed CLAUDE.md entry to one short clause; (2) further trimmed BUS0 sub-letter comment from 7 lines to 2 (matching neighbor `bus_`/`busn_` entries) — dropped CLAUDE.md-restating Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline reminder and the withdrawn-reservation history (commit log carries it). Out-of-scope deviations preserved with rationale: per-platform flavor-function extraction for steps 2/3 (current case-statement form already platform-branches on BURN_PLATFORM in zbujb_obliterate_workload + zbujb_garrison_step3_create; the docket's `no single-platform assumptions` done-when is met by existing structure; refactor would be stylistic, not load-bearing); w-inside-WSL elevation probe (current code routes inside-WSL ops via `wsl.exe --user root` bypassing sudo entirely — zero sudo -n callsites for inside-WSL to derive a probe-command list from; zbujp_probe_sudo takes a platform parameter so a future bubep_wsl_inner case is a small addition); mac sysadminctl -addUser -shell refactor (docket-locked but unverified across macOS versions; existing dscl . -create UserShell path already forces /bin/bash). Gates green throughout: bash -n on bujp_preflight.sh + bujb_jurisdiction.sh + bujb_cli.sh; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh 5 fixtures / 28 cases; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 4 fixtures / 98 cases (handbook-render exercises BUS0-included docs structurally so BUS0's reshape under the new quoin is gate-verified). Heat returns to: BUJP exists as the precondition layer; garrison-b is structurally ready for linux/mac empirical exercise (the b-letter platform-branching is in place); the next-actionable pace ₢A-AA1 (empirical-garrison-w-workload-wsl) will be the first end-to-end verification site for BUJP's probe paths against a real BURN target. Pre-existing gap not addressed: BUJB itself is missing from CLAUDE.md's BUK Subdirectory acronym map (not caused by this pace; small future itch slate).

ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS comment block rewritten to reflect that the dominant BatchMode=yes/ConnectTimeout=15 pair now lives in tinder constants (variant cases — BatchMode=no for password-fallback, ConnectTimeout=10 for knock — stay inline at their lone call sites). lastPrompt: Hold on, didn't we eliminate fenestrate?  I thought I saw that go by in a parallel chat.

content.text:
Fenestrate is half-eliminated in a specific way:

**In the spec** — yes, fenestrate as a verb is gone. BUSJPF-Fenestrate.adoc was absorbed into BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc as "Phase 1 (SSH-trust hardening)". The paddock confirms: "introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (**since broadened to caparison**)".

**In the bash code** — still very much present:
- `bujb_fenestrate` function in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
- `buw-jpF.Fenestrate.sh` tabtarget
- `bujb_fenestrate_command` dispatcher in `bujb_cli.sh`
- `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` handbook text mentions fenestrate ~13 times
- `bujp_preflight.sh` (just minted today) says "fenestrate covers Windows-admin elevation"

This gap is **exactly what ₢A-AA5 is for**. The docket's boundary line "`bujb_fenestrate` stays `bujb_fenestrate`" is load-bearing — the intent is to leave bash names alone (grep-archaeology) and bind them to the new spec via comment headers, like:

```bash
# Implements caparison-windows §SSH-trust phases (Phase 1 + Phase 2);
# see BUSJCW. Symbol retained from prior fenestrate verb for grep continuity.
bujb_fenestrate() { ... }
```

So your parallel chat eliminated fenestrate-as-spec-verb; the bash-rename pass was deliberately deferred. That said — if your intent has now shifted toward also renaming the bash symbols (rather than just annotating), this pace would need a redocket. Which posture do you want?

message.content: I wanted fenestrate deleted once subsumed by new command!

Let me get a clean inventory of what's actually present so we can scope the deletion.

lastPrompt: I wanted fenestrate deleted once subsumed by new command!

content.text:
Here's what's actually true on disk right now:

**Spec state**: BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc subsumes fenestrate as Phase 1+2, and adds Phase 3 (WSL stage, powercfg, Tailscale auto-start). BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows.adoc exists. BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc was just translated (₢A-AA2 commit).

**Bash state**: No `bujb_caparison_windows`, no `bujb_invigilate_windows`, no `buw-jpC*` or `buw-jpI*` tabtargets exist. The bash side has only:
- `bujb_fenestrate` + 7 `zbujb_fenestrate_*` helpers (~250 lines in bujb_jurisdiction.sh)
- `bujb_fenestrate_command` dispatcher in bujb_cli.sh
- `buw-jpF.Fenestrate.sh` tabtarget
- 13 fenestrate refs in buhj_jurisdiction.sh handbook text
- Zipper enrollment in buwz_zipper.sh
- One ref in rbhw0_top.sh

**The gap**: Spec subsumed fenestrate, but the bash subsumption — implementing caparison-windows (which would include fenestrate's phases 1+2 plus new phase 3) — was never done. Fenestrate-the-bash-symbol IS still the only working implementation of caparison-windows phases 1+2.

So "delete fenestrate" can't be a pure delete; the work is one of:

1. **Rename + extend** — `bujb_fenestrate` → `bujb_caparison_windows`, body augmented to include phase 3 (WSL stage, powercfg, Tailscale); add `buw-jpCW` tabtarget; delete `buw-jpF`; rewrite handbook text. Largest scope, but matches spec.
2. **Rename only** — `bujb_fenestrate` → `bujb_caparison_windows`, body unchanged (phases 1+2 only, phase 3 left as TODO). Mid-scope.
3. **Hide-not-delete** — drop the public dispatcher + tabtarget + handbook text; keep `bujb_fenestrate` as a private helper that the future `bujb_caparison_windows` will call. Smallest scope, defers the spec/code reconciliation.

This is a slate-time call — pace scope and segmentation are yours. Which posture should I prepare? (And: does ₢A-AA5 then evaporate, get redocketed to cover this, or stay as a follow-on for the non-fenestrate symbols once the rename lands?)

content.text:
Yes. **Caparison and invigilate exist only in the spec — never in bash.**

All 204 references to "caparison" or "invigilate" in the repo are inside `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/` (the AsciiDoc specs). Zero hits in any `.sh` file, tabtarget, handbook module, zipper, or CLAUDE.md.

The spec heat cycle authored the BUSJ family (10 sub-spec files spanning Caparison/Invigilate/Handbook/Garrison × Windows/Mac/Linux) — but the bash implementation never followed. `bujb_fenestrate` is still the only existing code for what the spec now calls caparison-windows phases 1+2, and there's no code at all for phase 3 (WSL stage / powercfg / Tailscale) or for any invigilate verb.

So the heat sequence in the paddock has spec-author paces complete but a bash-implementation pace either missing or not yet slated. ₢A-AA5's "comment annotations" framing presupposes a bash implementation that doesn't exist yet — which is why the docket reads strangely against your actual intent.

content.text:
Investigated. **The process hole is real and specific.**

## Pace inventory for ₣A-

**60 total paces; 32 complete, 25 abandoned, 4 remaining (all rough).**

The 4 remaining paces:

| Coronet | Silks | Posture |
|---|---|---|
| ₢A-AA1 | empirical-garrison-w-workload-wsl | Exercise garrison-w end-to-end |
| ₢A-AAq | bcg-repair-bujb-cli | BCG compliance touch on dispatcher |
| ₢A-AAx | practice-windows-jurisdiction-end-to-end | Operator Windows-host practice |
| ₢A-AA5 | bash-spec-link-comments | Comment annotations only |

**None of these implement caparison or invigilate in bash.**

## The hole

The spec refactor sequence (chronological):

1. **₢A-AAm** *implement-jurisdiction* — landed fenestrate + garrison in bash. Done.
2. **₢A-AA8** *bus0-caparison-invigilate-skeleton* — spec only. New vocabulary mints caparison + invigilate as distinct verbs, fenestrate folded into caparison-windows phase 1+2 with phase 3 added.
3. **₢A-AA7** *caparison-invigilate-windows-content* — spec only. Body-fills BUSJCW/BUSJIW/BUSJHW.
4. **₢A-AA6** *caparison-invigilate-mac-linux-content* — spec only. Body-fills BUSJC{M,L}/BUSJI{M,L}/BUSJH{M,L}/BUSJGB.
5. **₢A-AA2** *redesign-garrison-w-workload-owns-wsl* — touched bujb_jurisdiction.sh, but garrison-side only.
6. **₢A-AAy** *garrison-flavors-and-preflight* — minted bujp_preflight.sh. Garrison-side only.

After step 1, no bash-implementation pace exists for the new caparison or invigilate verbs. The three spec paces (8/7/6) had no slated companion implementation paces, and the subsequent paces (2, y) worked on garrison and preflight, leaving the caparison/invigilate verbs unimplemented.

The remaining 4 paces presuppose these verbs exist:
- ₢A-AA1 wants to run garrison-w empirically — but bujp_preflight (step 1 of garrison) is supposed to call invigilate-{platform} per the spec, and no `bujb_invigilate_*` exists.
- ₢A-AA5 wants to add comment headers binding bash symbols to new spec sections — but the symbols for caparison/invigilate don't exist.
- ₢A-AAx wants to practice end-to-end Windows jurisdiction — but caparison-windows-phase-3 (WSL stage, powercfg, Tailscale auto-start) has no bash code.

## What's missing

Minimally, three implementation paces never got slated:

- **implement-caparison-{windows,mac,linux}** — three platforms, with Windows being the largest (subsumes fenestrate phases 1+2, adds phase 3). New tabtargets `buw-jpC{W,M,L}`, new dispatcher entries, handbook text updates, fenestrate deletion (function + tabtarget + zipper + buhj_jurisdiction.sh references).
- **implement-invigilate-{windows,mac,linux}** — three platforms, read-only posture verification. New tabtargets `buw-jpI{W,M,L}`. bujp_preflight should call these per spec.
- **wire-preflight-to-invigilate** — once invigilate exists, bujp_preflight's probes should delegate to the invigilate verb rather than carrying inline platform-specific checks. (Or this stays inside the caparison/invigilate implementation paces.)

Optionally a fourth: **rewrite-buhj-jurisdiction-handbook** — 13 fenestrate refs in buhj_jurisdiction.sh need to be rephrased around caparison/invigilate verb vocabulary.

## Diagnosis

The spec authoring loop ran three paces (8/7/6) without slating their implementation counterparts. The "bash alignment via comment annotations" line in the paddock's Heat Sequence describes ₢A-AA5 — that line presupposes the bash already-implemented state, and was authored at a time when fenestrate-as-temporary-name was the intended bridge. But the actual gap is much deeper than annotations: there's no caparison-windows code, no invigilate code at all, and no garrison-side preconditions wired to invigilate.

Want me to draft the missing implementation paces, or do you want to slate them yourself first?

lastPrompt: Can you investigate the paces defined for this heat?  This seems like a massive process hole: I thought we had all implementation paces defined and even implemented.

message.content: chivvy paces for all missing implementation.  Should we do one pace per function or all at once?  Or batches?

content.text:
**Recommendation: 3 paces** in dependency order:

1. **implement-invigilate-all** — Build `bujb_invigilate_{windows,mac,linux}` per BUSJI{W,M,L} (read-only posture verifiers, smallest verbs, mechanical from spec); rewire `bujp_preflight` to delegate platform probes. First because preflight already wants to call this and garrison-w needs it as step 1.
2. **implement-caparison-windows** — Build `bujb_caparison_windows` per BUSJCW (Phase 1+2 = current fenestrate body, Phase 3 = new WSL stage + powercfg + Tailscale); delete fenestrate symbols (function, dispatcher, `buw-jpF`, zipper, 13 handbook refs in buhj_jurisdiction.sh, ref in rbhw0_top.sh). Largest pace; standalone to keep size honest.
3. **implement-caparison-mac-linux** — Build `bujb_caparison_{macos,linux}` per BUSJC{M,L} (small ssh-copy-id orchestration verbs, parallel structure). Mirrors the spec-author pace ₢A-AA6 which bundled M+L.

**Tradeoff vs. alternatives**:
- *One pace per function (~9 paces)*: too granular — caparison-windows + fenestrate-deletion can't cleanly separate (intermediate state would have two parallel SSH-trust paths), and invigilate-windows orphaned without preflight rewire is incoherent.
- *All at once*: 500+ lines of new bash + deletion + handbook would blow size guard and bury distinct decision points.
- *Per-platform (W / M / L)*: would force each pace to mix caparison-verb work with invigilate-verb work and preflight-wiring; verb-batching keeps each pace's conceptual scope unified.

**On ₢A-AA5**: once these three land, bash symbols match the spec by construction — `bash-spec-link-comments` becomes mostly obsolete. Worth marking it for a re-evaluation (keep, drop, or shrink to whatever residual non-caparison/invigilate annotations remain) after pace 3.

**On chivvy order**: each chivvy goes to position 1, so to land in the order above (invigilate first), I'd chivvy them in reverse: caparison-mac-linux, then caparison-windows, then invigilate-all. After chivvying, the queue becomes: invigilate-all → caparison-windows → caparison-mac-linux → ₢A-AA1 → ₢A-AAq → ₢A-AAx → ₢A-AA5.

Want me to proceed with these three, or adjust the segmentation first?
Drafting and chivvying in reverse order so they land in dependency order.

# jjezs_slate implement-invigilate-all

## Character
Mechanical translation — BUSJI{W,M,L} are locked; read-only posture verifiers.

## Goal
Build invigilate-{windows,macos,linux} verbs per BUSJI{W,M,L}, with `buw-jpI{W,M,L}` tabtargets. Rewire bujp_preflight to delegate platform probes to the new verbs.

## Boundary
- Invigilate is read-only: no mutations, no remediation. Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic with operator-handbook vs caparison pointer per spec.
- Platform probes currently inline in bujp_preflight (sudo NOPASSWD, admin-group, WSL distribution) move under the invigilate verb that owns them; bujp becomes verb-delegation.

## Done when
- buw-jpI{W,M,L} exit 0 against a correctly-configured node; exit non-zero with fact-named diagnostic against a misconfigured node.
- bujp_preflight calls invigilate-{platform} at step 1 of garrison; zero inline posture probes remain.
- bash -n; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh.

content.text:
The dockets are sophisticated; here's the audit:

**Cleanup surface, verified:**
- Spec-side fenestrate is already gone (BUSJPF deleted, BUS0 clean, zero `.adoc` hits, zero CLAUDE.md hits).
- All 86 residual fenestrate hits live in 6 bash files: `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, `bujb_cli.sh`, `bujp_preflight.sh`, `buhj_jurisdiction.sh`, `buwz_zipper.sh`, `rbhw0_top.sh` — plus the `buw-jpF` tabtarget.

**Pace-by-pace cleanup scope:**

- **₢A-ABD invigilate-all** — no prior draft exists; cleanup scope is the bujp_preflight rewire (inline probes → verb delegation), which is in the docket. ✓
- **₢A-ABC caparison-windows** — owns the full fenestrate deletion. Docket lists the surfaces (function family, dispatcher, tabtarget, zipper, handbook refs in both `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` and `rbhw0_top.sh`) and gates with `grep -ri fenestrate Tools/ tt/ returns zero hits`. That gate also catches the fenestrate refs inside `bujp_preflight.sh` and `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` even though I didn't enumerate every file by name. ✓
- **₢A-ABB caparison-mac-linux** — pure net-add; no prior draft to remove. ✓

**Recommendation: no reslate, no distinct deletion paces.**

Reason: splitting `delete-fenestrate` out of caparison-windows would create an awkward intermediate state (both functions live, both dispatchers wired, both in handbook prose) that doesn't bisect cleanly. The fenestrate body becomes caparison-windows phases 1+2 by direct re-homing — they're the same bytes, not two implementations. Keeping the transition atomic preserves bisectability and avoids carrying obsolete code between commits.

**One latitude to note**: the `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` handbook rewrite (13 fenestrate refs embedded in operator-facing explanation) could turn out to be larger than a mechanical search-replace if the conceptual flow needs reorganizing. If at mount time it becomes its own animal, the mount agent can spike a follow-on slate (e.g., `rewrite-buhj-caparison-vocabulary`) and narrow caparison-windows to the code-only deletion. Default: keep it bundled.

Want to mount ₢A-ABD now, or any further docket adjustments first?

message.content: Can we do any of these paces in parallel for speed?

content.text:
Parallel possible but with friction. Here's the honest mapping:

**File-touch overlap** (every pace touches the three core files):

| File | ABD invigilate | ABC caparison-W | ABB caparison-ML |
|---|---|---|---|
| `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` | add | add+delete fenestrate | add |
| `bujb_cli.sh` | add | add+delete | add |
| `buwz_zipper.sh` | add | add+delete | add |
| `bujp_preflight.sh` | rewire step 1 | rewrite fenestrate refs | — |
| `buhj_jurisdiction.sh` | — | rewrite 13 refs | — |
| `rbhw0_top.sh` | — | rewrite | — |
| `tt/buw-jp*` | add I{W,M,L} | add CW, delete F | add C{M,L} |

**Semantic dependencies:**
- ABC and ABB both end with a `bujb_invigilate_{platform}` post-check call. Runtime dep on ABD, not write-time — the symbol just needs to exist before the pace is *executed*, not before its commit lands.
- ABC deletes fenestrate from files ABD and ABB are adding to. Hardest merge surface.

**Recommendation: two-officium parallelism (ABD ∥ ABB, then ABC sequentially).**

- **ABD ∥ ABB** is clean: both are pure net-add (no deletions), both mechanical from spec, both touch core files in different function-definition regions. Rebase-merging is mechanical "additions in different parts of the file." Merge ABD first so invigilate symbols exist before ABB's caparison-{mac,linux} call them.
- **ABC sequentially after** — the fenestrate deletion mid-pace mutates the same regions ABD/ABB were adding to. Running ABC concurrently amplifies rebase pain and risks leaving fenestrate/caparison-windows both alive in some intermediate commit. Sequential keeps it atomic and bisectable.

**Why not full three-way parallel:**
ABC's deletion overlap with ABD and ABB makes the merge non-mechanical. You'd save maybe 30–60 min on wall-clock and spend it on rebase reconciliation across the three core files. Net wash, plus a bigger chance of an "intermediate broken commit" between merges.

**Why not sequential all three:**
ABD and ABB share zero semantic content (one defines invigilate verbs, the other defines caparison verbs for non-Windows platforms). The parallel speedup is real and the merge is mechanical. Worth taking.

**To execute parallelism**: a second chat session opens its own officium (`jjx_open`), mounts the other pace, works on its own branch or local commits. When both complete, merge ABD first, then ABB (which sees ABD's invigilate symbols already in place), then proceed with ABC.

Want to proceed with ABD here (mounting now) while you spawn a parallel session for ABB? Or keep it sequential?

content.content:
6b505a5f jjb:1011-6e7a47c6:₢A-AA2:n: Garrison-w refactor: workload owns its WSL distribution. BUSJGW spec redrafted to the three-namespace shape (Windows account + per-user HKCU\Lxss distribution registration + WSL-Linux user inside the workload-owned distribution); the per-user-WSL constraint is forced by Microsoft's HKCU\Lxss-only registration semantics. Mechanism is SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged orchestrator: orchestrator places a bare authorized_keys (no command= directive), opens an SSH session as bujuw_user — a real Windows logon with HKCU mounted per cycle-9's profile registration — and from inside that session runs wsl --import (writing rbtww-main into the workload's HKCU naturally), inner-WSL useradd + passwd --lock, and privkey plant into the workload's distribution; the bare entry is then replaced with the locked-down command= form by a second SSH-as-workload session that overwrites the workload-owned authorized_keys file. Implementation in bujb_jurisdiction.sh adds zbujb_workload_ssh (cmd.exe-routed transport mirror of zbujb_admin_powershell), zbujb_w_init_run wrapper (per-call diag capture under ZBUJB_W_INIT_PREFIX), and four new w-specific step functions (zbujb_garrison_w_export_seed using admin PS for wsl --export to a workload-readable seed at C:\Users\<workload>\rbtww-seed.tar; zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl driving the four-call SSH-as-workload sequence; zbujb_garrison_w_lockdown rewriting authorized_keys via wsl --user root bash on workload's freshly-imported distribution; zbujb_garrison_w_seed_cleanup removing the seed tarball post-import). Step 3 w branch trimmed to net.exe + profile registration only — the Linux user creation moved into init_wsl inside the workload's distribution. Step 4 w branch writes a bare authorized_keys (no command= directive) so the SSH-as-workload session can drop into a normal cmd.exe shell; b/c letters keep their locked-down command= directive in step 4. bujb_garrison() dispatcher routes w letter through the new export/init/lockdown/cleanup sequence, skipping the b/c step5_plant_key (which the inner privkey plant subsumes for w). BUHJ handbook in zbuhj_render_post_bootstrap notes admin's rbtww-main as the seed source and the per-user-WSL constraint that forces it; operator keeps admin's rbtww-main pristine since customizations propagate to workload via the seed. WSG gains SH-9 ('single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport') as the negative form of PS-3, with a transport-stack quote-form decision table — this rule was empirical: my first draft of the wsl --import call used '${z_seed_win}' single-quoted (matching the export-seed call's PS-routed pattern), but cmd.exe-direct transport sends the single quotes through to wsl.exe's argv as literal characters, breaking path resolution; \"...\" with bash escape is the cmd.exe-direct equivalent. BCG-compliance pass on new bash: split local from $() captures; single-line for $(<file) reads per BCG line 466; |\| buc_die after every external command and zbujb_w_init_run wrapper. Spec text adjusted in the lockdown step to say 'rewrite happens from a workload SSH session' (was 'from the admin session') because the file is workload-owned post-step-4 icacls and workload-driven write avoids ownership transfer gymnastics. Gates: bash -n green on both modified .sh files; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh 5 fixtures 28 cases all passing; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 4 fixtures 98 cases all passing. Empirical end-to-end deferred to the next pace per docket.
d65dee17 jjb:1011-cf6082b6:₣A-:n: Add 'Windows: Host Availability (optional)' section to BUK jurisdiction handbook between Linux/Mac note and existing sshd-reachability bootstrap. Covers physically-secured single-occupant rooms that need power-on autonomy: Tailscale install (default per-user auto-start), Run-Unattended mode via tray-icon Preferences (with the issue-#3186 first-boot caveat — interactive login once before unattended mode settles), and Windows auto-login via netplwiz (Win+R, uncheck the password-required checkbox, enter password twice) plus the registry sub-step for Microsoft-account hosts where the checkbox is hidden (set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device\DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion = 0). Uses buh_step1/buh_step2 macros so step numbering auto-increments and flows into the existing sshd-reachability section (now steps 4-8 instead of 1-5; previously hardcoded). Section is presented as optional with a Precondition block warning that auto-login removes the keyboard barrier (physical access becomes total access). Bootstrap section's Preconditions gains a cross-reference: 'for unattended power-on, see Windows: Host Availability above'. New render fn zbuhj_render_windows_availability called from buhj_top after zbuhj_render_linux_mac_note. No new tabtarget, no zipper entry, no new file — content surfaces automatically through the existing BUWZ_HJ0_TOP reference in the RBK Windows orchestrator's Phase 1.

ddb9fd9f jjb:1011-d86518ca:₢A-AAo:n: Add 'Enable Password Authentication in sshd_config' step to Windows handbook procedure (between Start/Enable Service and Allow Firewall Port). Empirical finding from rocket bootstrap: Windows OpenSSH 9.5 ships with sshd_config in a state that blocks password auth — the wire protocol advertises keyboard-interactive (the standard wrapper for password delivery on Windows) but the server refuses to actually prompt, producing 'Permission denied (publickey,keyboard-interactive)' with no prompt ever shown to the operator. Microsoft Learn confirms: on Windows OpenSSH the only configurable AuthenticationMethods are 'password' and 'publickey' (KbdInteractiveAuthentication is in the not-available list); the server's PasswordAuthentication directive controls both wire methods. Default-shipped state empirically rejects password auth even when the wire protocol advertises it. New step uses notepad on BUBC_windows_sshd_config (operator already has elevated PowerShell so SYSTEM-owned write succeeds), instructs operator to set PasswordAuthentication yes (or add the line if absent), then Restart-Service sshd. Step prose explains this is temporary state — garrison flips back to 'no' as part of its hardening. Placement is post-Start/Enable (sshd_config doesn't exist until first sshd start) and pre-Firewall (so the server is in the correct state when first reachable from network). Step 3 (Start/Enable) gains a one-line note that first start generates the default sshd_config. Reused BUBC_windows_sshd_config (had been retired from handbook in earlier trim, now re-adopted for this step — single source of truth preserved). Validated via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now reads 5 numbered steps (Set Password / Install / Start+Enable / Enable PasswordAuth / Firewall) followed by Verify Reachability and Run Garrison sections.
e170740c jjb:1011-2615224d:₢A-AAo:n: Add explicit 'set or confirm admin password' step to handbook — don't presume operator already knows the local admin password (Windows logon is often via PIN/Hello/Microsoft-account; Linux admin user may have no password set). Windows: new Step 1 (Set or Confirm Admin Password) using `net user <admin-user> <temp-password>`, with note that the value stops affecting SSH after garrison hardens sshd_config; existing 3 steps renumber to 2/3/4 via auto-counter. Dropped redundant 'Admin user account has a known password' precondition bullet (now covered by step). Linux/Mac: parallel guidance added — `sudo passwd <admin-user>` if unsure of the password, with same 'value stops affecting SSH after garrison runs' framing. Operators who prefer pre-establishing key trust can still use ssh-copy-id (kept as alternative). Verified via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now 4 numbered steps + Verify Reachability + Run Garrison; Linux/Mac section reads cleanly as two paragraphs (manual scope + password setup) plus the optional ssh-copy-id alternative. Landing prose unchanged — 'make sshd reachable on the network with a known admin password' is now backed by explicit guidance for ensuring that password is known.
2615224d jjb:1011-bc4ed03f:₢A-AAo:n: Land dual-purpose-garrison pivot in BUSJGB and trim handbook to manual minimum. BUSJGB amended: dropped 'operator-bootstrapped out-of-band' precondition, replaced with PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password contract (garrison never sees the password — ssh prompts on /dev/tty); reframed NOTE as dual-purpose (front-half steps 1-4 idempotent admin trust + sshd harden, back-half steps 5-9 destructive workload). Two new front-half steps inserted between original step 2 (place admin trust) and step 3 (destroy workload): chmod admin authorized_keys; harden sshd_config (PubkeyAuthentication yes, PasswordAuthentication no, PermitEmptyPasswords no) + validate + restart sshd. Idempotency wording on each front-half step so retries converge. Guarantee + completion clauses extended for sshd hardening. buhj_jurisdiction.sh trimmed: module header rewritten (garrison handles admin trust, not operator); landing renderer flips operator-vs-garrison narrative; Linux/Mac note kept trivial but trimmed of the 'if password disallowed' edge case (no longer relevant under password-fallback); Windows bootstrap collapsed from 9 steps to 3 (Install OpenSSH Server / Start+Enable Service / Allow Firewall Port) + admin-password precondition + Verify Reachability section (its own buh_section to break out of step-3 indent); post-bootstrap renderer mentions first-run password prompt; buc_doc_brief updated; kindle precondition retargeted from BUBC_windows_admin_auth_keys to BUBC_windows_ssh_port (the two retired BUBC vars are no longer rendered in the trim but left in bubc_constants.sh — implement-jurisdiction will need them in the garrison itself). Paddock §Current Concept / §Garrison ceremony / §Standing Notes already matched the new model from prior currying commit d7127ac5; no paddock work needed. Out-of-docket drift spotted but NOT touched: BUSJGC:20 + BUSJGW:25 still carry the dropped 'operator-bootstrapped out-of-band' wording, and BUS0:1107-1109 ('Garrison authenticates using this key only — there is no password fallback. First-time admin trust is established out-of-band...') + BUS0:1462-1463 ('first-time admin SSH bootstrap is an out-of-band handbook procedure') contradict the new model; flagged for a follow-up pace. Verified: tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh renders cleanly (landing → Linux/Mac → Windows 3-step → Verify Reachability → Run Garrison).
f3c25bcd jjb:1011-4e890ee5:₢A-AAo:n: Land Windows admin SSH bootstrap handbook under jurisdiction. New buhj_jurisdiction.sh module renders BUSJH0 landing + Linux/Mac trivial note + 9-step Windows bootstrap (sshd install, key-only sshd_config hardening, bare admin pubkey placement, icacls lockdown, key-auth verification) + post-bootstrap garrison pointers from a single buw-hj0 tabtarget. Windows OpenSSH layout strings (sshd_config path, administrators_authorized_keys path, port 22, firewall rule + display name) minted in bubc_constants.sh as BUBC_windows_* tinder — single source of truth, shared with future bujb_jurisdiction.sh when implement-jurisdiction lands. buhj_cli.sh thin furnish parallels buhw_cli.sh and burp_cli.sh; sources bubc_constants.sh + buym_yelp + buh_handbook + buhj. buwz_zipper.sh gains a jurisdiction-handbook section enrolling BUWZ_HJ0_TOP. Garrison-destructive model honored throughout: operator places BARE admin pubkey only (NO command= directive) because garrison rewrites authorized_keys with the shell-letter command= on first run; warning prose makes this load-bearing. Content salvaged from buhw_access_base (sshd install + lockdown) + the icacls portion of buhw_access_entrypoints; buhw_access_remote dropped as it conflicted with bus_keys_operator_owned (operator owns key generation; not a handbook concern). buhw_windows.sh and its five buw-HW* tabtargets retained intact for AAn-scope retirement; the new content lives alongside, not on top of, them. buh_index_buk gains a Jurisdiction pointer line. Latent bug surfaced separately during smoke-test (pre-existing at HEAD, not introduced here): BUWZ_* colophon vars are populated by zbuwz_kindle in the workbench process via printf -v which doesn't export, so they're unbound after buz_exec_lookup execs into the CLI process; buw-h0 (via buh_index_buk's BUWZ_HW_TOP ref) and buw-hw (via buhw_top's BUWZ_RHC_LINUX ref) both fail with unbound-variable errors at HEAD before any of these changes. My buh_index_buk addition adds a BUWZ_HJ0_TOP reference of the same shape — same bug, not a new one. buw-hj0 itself renders cleanly because it never traverses buh_index_buk. BCG question handled: Windows path strings are pure literals with no runtime dependency, so they're tinder (lowercase body, no readonly, declared at module top) rather than kindle constants — buhw_windows.sh's existing pattern of declaring them as ZBUHW_* readonly inside kindle is suboptimal but out of scope for retirement-bound code. Operator-on-Windows test still pending (cannot be done from this session); buw-h0 latent-bug resolution still pending decision.

6b505a5f jjb:1011-6e7a47c6:₢A-AA2:n: Garrison-w refactor: workload owns its WSL distribution. BUSJGW spec redrafted to the three-namespace shape (Windows account + per-user HKCU\Lxss distribution registration + WSL-Linux user inside the workload-owned distribution); the per-user-WSL constraint is forced by Microsoft's HKCU\Lxss-only registration semantics. Mechanism is SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged orchestrator: orchestrator places a bare authorized_keys (no command= directive), opens an SSH session as bujuw_user — a real Windows logon with HKCU mounted per cycle-9's profile registration — and from inside that session runs wsl --import (writing rbtww-main into the workload's HKCU naturally), inner-WSL useradd + passwd --lock, and privkey plant into the workload's distribution; the bare entry is then replaced with the locked-down command= form by a second SSH-as-workload session that overwrites the workload-owned authorized_keys file. Implementation in bujb_jurisdiction.sh adds zbujb_workload_ssh (cmd.exe-routed transport mirror of zbujb_admin_powershell), zbujb_w_init_run wrapper (per-call diag capture under ZBUJB_W_INIT_PREFIX), and four new w-specific step functions (zbujb_garrison_w_export_seed using admin PS for wsl --export to a workload-readable seed at C:\Users\<workload>\rbtww-seed.tar; zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl driving the four-call SSH-as-workload sequence; zbujb_garrison_w_lockdown rewriting authorized_keys via wsl --user root bash on workload's freshly-imported distribution; zbujb_garrison_w_seed_cleanup removing the seed tarball post-import). Step 3 w branch trimmed to net.exe + profile registration only — the Linux user creation moved into init_wsl inside the workload's distribution. Step 4 w branch writes a bare authorized_keys (no command= directive) so the SSH-as-workload session can drop into a normal cmd.exe shell; b/c letters keep their locked-down command= directive in step 4. bujb_garrison() dispatcher routes w letter through the new export/init/lockdown/cleanup sequence, skipping the b/c step5_plant_key (which the inner privkey plant subsumes for w). BUHJ handbook in zbuhj_render_post_bootstrap notes admin's rbtww-main as the seed source and the per-user-WSL constraint that forces it; operator keeps admin's rbtww-main pristine since customizations propagate to workload via the seed. WSG gains SH-9 ('single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport') as the negative form of PS-3, with a transport-stack quote-form decision table — this rule was empirical: my first draft of the wsl --import call used '${z_seed_win}' single-quoted (matching the export-seed call's PS-routed pattern), but cmd.exe-direct transport sends the single quotes through to wsl.exe's argv as literal characters, breaking path resolution; \"...\" with bash escape is the cmd.exe-direct equivalent. BCG-compliance pass on new bash: split local from $() captures; single-line for $(<file) reads per BCG line 466; |\| buc_die after every external command and zbujb_w_init_run wrapper. Spec text adjusted in the lockdown step to say 'rewrite happens from a workload SSH session' (was 'from the admin session') because the file is workload-owned post-step-4 icacls and workload-driven write avoids ownership transfer gymnastics. Gates: bash -n green on both modified .sh files; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh 5 fixtures 28 cases all passing; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 4 fixtures 98 cases all passing. Empirical end-to-end deferred to the next pace per docket.
